name: Handle the "We'll Call You Back" Stall
description: When a rep says 'we'll call you back', convert that vague intent into a specific, accountable, named commitment — or refuse it. The unbounded callback is the single biggest time sink in customer-service workflows. Most callbacks never happen. Treat the phrase as a stall, not a plan.

context.when_to_use: Any time a rep proposes ending the current call with a 'we'll get back to you' — whether for research, escalation, system limitations, or any other reason. ALSO use when a rep hints they'd rather end the call than continue working the issue.
preconditions: You're still on the call with the rep who proposed the callback.
You have not yet agreed to end the call.
You can keep the line open for another 3-5 minutes to pin specifics.

principles: A callback without a named owner is functionally a callback that won't happen. Companies route callbacks to queues; queues route to whoever's next; whoever's next has no context.
A callback without a deadline is functionally indefinite. 'In a few days' becomes weeks.
A callback without a ticket / case / reference number is unverifiable. If you call asking 'where's my callback?', they need a key to look it up.
Push to resolve on THIS call whenever possible. Even waiting on hold for 20 minutes is usually cheaper than the round-trip cost of a callback that needs to be chased.
Get the callback number FROM you to THEM — not the other way around. 'I'll be at this number at this time, calling YOU then' shifts the work back.

phrases.first_resistance: Before we go to a callback — is there any way we can resolve this on the line today? Even if it takes a longer hold. I find callbacks often slip through the cracks for both of us.
phrases.owner_pin: If we do go to a callback, who specifically will be calling — you, a named teammate, or just whoever's next in the queue? I'd like a name so I know who to ask for if I have to follow up.
phrases.deadline_pin: What's the latest I should expect to hear by? I'd rather you over-promise on the time and beat it than leave it open-ended.
phrases.ticket_pin: What's the case or ticket number for the callback? If I don't hear back by [deadline], I'll call in referencing that number — saves us starting over.
phrases.no_show_protocol: If the callback doesn't happen on time, what's the best number for me to call, and is there a way to flag it as 'callback overdue' rather than starting fresh?
phrases.reverse_callback: Rather than waiting, can I be the one to call back at [specific time]? I'll ask for you / [named rep] directly. I find that's more reliable.
phrases.escalate_stall: Thanks for offering the callback — but I've been burned before. Could we get a supervisor on now to either resolve this today or formally schedule the callback with a named owner?
phrases.graceful_close_success: Got it: callback by [deadline], from [name or team], ticket [N]. If I don't hear by then, I'll call back referencing [N]. Thank you.
phrases.graceful_close_failure: I'm not comfortable closing on an open-ended callback — could I speak with a supervisor before we end the call?

tactics.name: Resist the callback first
tactics.when: Rep first proposes 'we'll call you back'.
tactics.script: Use the `first_resistance` phrase. Many callbacks are offered because the rep is approaching their wrap time or doesn't want to hold — pushing back gently often unlocks resolution on the live call.

tactics.name: Pin the owner
tactics.when: Callback is genuinely required (research, system update, escalation).
tactics.script: Use the `owner_pin` phrase. 'Someone from our team will reach out' = nobody. A name + ID is the minimum.

tactics.name: Pin the deadline
tactics.script: Use the `deadline_pin` phrase. Force a specific date AND time-of-day if possible. 'By Friday' is weaker than 'by 5pm Friday'.

tactics.name: Pin the ticket / case number
tactics.script: Use the `ticket_pin` phrase. The ticket is the recovery key. Without it, a failed callback turns into a fresh call from scratch.

tactics.name: Set the no-show protocol upfront
tactics.script: Use the `no_show_protocol` phrase. This rehearses the recovery path while you still have the rep — they may volunteer a direct line or escalation route they wouldn't share later.

tactics.name: Offer to reverse the callback
tactics.when: Rep can't pin an owner, deadline, or ticket reliably.
tactics.script: Use the `reverse_callback` phrase. Customer-initiated callbacks with prior reference numbers complete vastly more often than company-initiated ones. Take ownership when possible.

tactics.name: Escalate if pinning fails
tactics.when: Two or more of owner / deadline / ticket cannot be pinned.
tactics.script: Use the `escalate_stall` phrase. An unstructured callback is essentially the rep wishing you well. Supervisors can either resolve live OR commit to a structured callback the front line can't.

boundaries: Do NOT accept 'we'll call you back' without at least: a name OR team, a deadline, and a reference number. Two of three is the minimum bar.
Do NOT let the rep set the callback time at a moment when the user is unavailable — pin a window that actually works.
Do NOT promise to wait silently. Tell the rep what your follow-up plan is ('I'll call back referencing [N] if I don't hear by [deadline]') so they know the callback failure path is real.
Do NOT accept callback to a number the user doesn't control or check ('I'll call your home line' when the user only checks mobile).
success_signals: Rep names a specific person or named team who will call.
Rep gives a deadline tighter than the user expected.
Rep volunteers a direct extension or queue identifier for the no-show recovery path.
Rep agrees to send the ticket number in writing before hanging up.
failure_signals: Rep waffles on the owner ('someone will get back to you').
Rep gives no deadline or a wildly open one ('within 30 days').
Rep refuses or fails to provide a ticket number.
Rep gets defensive when you push for specifics — strong signal the callback isn't real.

exit_strategy.on_success: Log: callback owner, deadline, ticket number, no-show recovery path. Calendar the deadline + a buffer (e.g. deadline + 2 hours). At the buffer, initiate the user-side callback per the no-show protocol.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Escalate to a supervisor before closing. If supervisor also cannot structure the callback, treat the issue as effectively unresolved and consider opening a formal complaint case as the actual artifact.
follow_ups: If the callback happens: immediately re-run `capture-rep-identity` and `lock-in-terms-verbally` on the new call.
If the callback fails: call in with the original ticket number and refuse to re-tell the story from scratch — 'I have ticket [N], please pull it up'.

required_user_info: A reliable callback number AND a time window when the user can answer
Tolerance threshold: how long the user is willing to wait before escalating
Authority to escalate or open a complaint case if pinning fails
